key_insight: In multiobjective topology optimization, Pareto fronts can be disconnected and nonconvex because they are composed of local front segments, and scalarization choice strongly affects how well the front is sampled.
engineering_relevance: For engineering design, this means trade-offs between objectives like compliance, stress, volume, and dynamic compliance may be missed or distorted unless a suitable scalarization strategy is used.
method: Compared weighted-sum and ε-constraint formulations against Pascoletti-Serafini scalarization.
Analyzed the theoretical structure of Pareto fronts in topology optimization.
Performed numerical bi-objective studies across multiple benchmark objectives to assess front coverage and distribution.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports a large set of bi-objective examples and a comparative numerical evaluation showing more uniform approximations with Pascoletti-Serafini and clustering with weighted-sum.
watch_item: Weighted-sum methods can miss disconnected or nonconvex regions of the Pareto front, so solver and scalarization choice can bias the apparent design trade-off.
paper_title: Novel insights into Pareto fronts in multiobjective topology optimization and a comparative study of scalarization strategies
